title: KDD 2025 - Keynote Speakers: Chris Manning / Meaning and ...
text:
Okay, thank you John for that lovely
introduction and hello everyone at KDD.
Um hope you've still got energy for the
last day of this conference. So what I
want to do today is both talk about um a
little bit of the history that's led to
this surprising victory of NLP and then
moving from some of those themes into
some of my recent work.
we actually are in this most
extraordinary moment
that it's you know the world is
happening around us but I I think it's
worth reflecting on just how dramatic
and sudden the developments of the last
few years have been um one way of
looking at this is how dramatically the
corporate world has changed since the
release of chat GPT so chat GPT was
released
at the very end of 2022
and that set off a absolute fury of
interest in AI in the world at large.
And one way of looking at that is for
the US S&P 500 largest companies that in
their quarterly earnings calls for the
last five quarters straight over 40% of
companies each time have mentioned AI
during their quarterly earnings call.
Another graph that was um recently um
doing the rounds on social media of
dramatic change is this one contrasting
office construction versus data center
construction in the United States. So
office construction has tanked. Um
that's largely been for other reasons in
the um follow on from the co 19 pandemic
um with the move to having less in
office work. Um, but for the AI side of
things, we see that again since the
launch of Chat GPT, there's just been
this enormous boom in constructing data
centers. Enormous money is going into
infrastructure development predicated on
the technological breakthroughs of LLMs.
And so that's the topic I want to deal
with today. But I am still sensitive to
this issue of who should you take your
large language model wisdom from in
2025.
Um from me or perhaps someone younger
and more involved in building frontier
large language models like Jason Wei. I
don't mean this question facitiously. I
mean for there are tons of questions for
the inner details of training LLMs for
which someone like Jason Weey could give
a better answer than I could. So I
thought I should begin with some more
historical reflections um where my
wisdom and experience count for more
since I think something that we should
reflect on for a few minutes. It is
actually quite extraordinary and
unexpected based on the history of AI
how things have developed in the last um
few years. So I think the overall story
of the renaissance of neural networks is
fairly wellnown. It crystallized um
through initially work in speech
recognition in 2009 and then object
recognition in images on Faith Al's
imageet data set in 2012. Both of these
pieces of work um were led by Jeff
Hinton and his students right here in
Toronto. Um the latter became the seinal
breakthrough AI event, but it's actually
the former that had much more immediate
commercial impact. Microsoft and Google
quickly scaled up that work and that's
what led to speech recognition on phones
as we have it today that actually works.
Um so already we see a form of language
understanding prominent in the deep
learning revolution.
But from there a decade later we got
into the world of large language models.
And large language models have become so
dominant that they're basically in the
popular press synonymous with artificial
intelligence these days. If you see an
article that's talking about the rise of
artificial intelligence, it's going to
be talking about large language models.
So for these models, you feed in a ton
of text into an enormous neural network.
um it abstracts over them with numeric
parameters and then it can produce
coherent fresh text of amazing quality
by simply generating one token after
another in a sequence. And this has led
to language use in LLMs not only
becoming the new interaction language
between humans and computers but rapidly
for it also becoming the new highle
programming language that people are
using.
So all current state-of-the-art
transformers are built oh sorry trans
state-of-the-art LLMs are built on the
transformer neural network architecture.
Um the transforming neural network
architecture is from a paper from Google
in 2017 and this paper was entitled
attention is all you need. And so
underlying that was the key development
of this new concept of neural attention.
first by researchers Dimma now and
colleagues at the University of Montreal
and then a second version of attention a
year later by my student Tang Luong and
me um at Stanford and it's really that
second version of attention that became
the basis of the version of attention
used in transformers
um and this new model of language
processing with attention and
transformers
scaled up has just produced spectacular
results. And so we can ask our large
language models to do crazy things. So
we can give them a task like write a set
praising Wikipedia in which each line
begins with the letter W and give it a
second and chat GPT40 will come back
with something like this. Um, with
wisdom vast it spans the world so wide,
wielding the wealth of knowledge free to
share. Words weave through time where
curious minds confide. Whispering truths
with patience, just and fair. I'll skip
some of it. Um, wisdom and will sustain
this noble dream. Wikipedia, our light,
our endless stream. Um,
you know, I'm sure all of you have
played with CE GPT and seen it can do
wonderful things. But I mean, again, I
do just want to say this is
extraordinary, right? As someone who's
been doing this NLP stuff for 30 years,
the idea that you can just give a
computer an instruction and you know
what is at heart a very simple neural
network operation of use context protect
predict next token and then repeat could
be successful at doing this is quite
extraordinary. I mean, you know, so I
teach all about transformers and
pre-training and post-training and RLHF
and all of that stuff in CS224N,
but it's still kind of hard to explain
how you can predict tokens one at a time
and yet it could do this correctly where
in particular, what is this? Well, if I
deviate into the English lesson for a
few moments in case you're a bit rusty
on this. So a sonnet's a 14line poem and
it has a rhyming scheme of abab except
at the end where the two final lines
rhyme. So wide confide share fair rhyme
etc down and then dream rhyme. Um
sonnetss are inamic pentameter with
wisdom vast it spans the world so wide
and these lines are all inic pentameter.
And then just to make it crazy
difficult, I added on this extra um
funny constraint that I wanted it to
begin every line with the letter W. And
it did. Um and somehow it was able to
put all of this together from a simple
instruction um and produce something
that well might not be the world's
finest poetry. I'll admit that. but as a
passible version of poetry. Um,
observing all the constraints of subject
matter, the form of a sonnet and my
crazy constraint of beginning each line
with the letter W. I mean, I think that
is actually nothing short of an
extraordinary technology breakthrough.
So, where did this come from and how did
we end up at this place? And I think the
second observation I want to make is,
you know, ending up here wasn't how
anyone thought AI was going to work or
how it was going to develop. So the
first conference I ever went to and
presented a paper um was ACL 1993.
So it was held in Columbus, Ohio at the
um the the Ohio State University. It was
held in a single lecture hall which held
about I don't know 200 people and at
conferences weren't like they are these
days. At that conference there were
precisely 33 um main conference papers
um spread over two and a half days. Um
this isn't an actually a photo of ACL
1993. Um I couldn't I don't have one of
those. Um, but I thought it could sort
of evoke kind of what a conference was
like those in those days. In particular,
it shows the then essential piece of
technology, overhead projectors, where
you'd print stuff out on a piece of
plastic um and um display it there. Um,
so here I was um in 1993 giving my first
um conference paper. Um, this was an
exceedingly exciting time in NLP cuz it
was just after digital text had started
to become widely available. You know,
the worldwide web kind of barely
existed. I guess that's the sort of
start of hypertext at CERN, but there
started to be enough digital text and
that meant you could start to do
empirical natural language processing
work and build models. And indeed there
I was as a young graduate student and my
paper was doing what we call statistical
NLP working from empirical NLP data
making models. Um now the question then
is that of these 33 papers that were at
um ACL 1993 how many of them were about
language models? Um and the answer to
that question is zero. Um if I if I ask
a slightly more broader qualifying
question and say of these 33 papers how
many of these papers did language model
appear in as a biogram the answer to
that is two um that's these two papers
so Pereira Tishbian Lee presented a
paper on distributional clustering
methods for English words and they
suggested that this technique could be
useful
for building class-based language
models, avoiding some of the sparseness
that comes from word counts. Um, and the
group at IBM, which I'll come back to in
a minute, um, led by Fred Gelanick, um,
they presented a paper towards
historybased grammarss using richer
models for probabilistic paring. And
interestingly that was arguing that
there were too many limitations of
engram language models for language
understanding. And so we needed to
pursue richer models like grammar-based
models and probabilistic parsing to get
further in natural language
understanding.
But you know there were zero papers that
were actually building language models
with 20 with 2020 hindsight. That seems
really weird and wrong as if the whole
field was going off in what turned out
to be ultimately a wrong direction
because it's not exactly you know why
was no one working on language models
it's not exactly like it was a new
technology as I'll explain in a moment
so you know how were they hoping to get
to artificial intelligence so the
central
observation is that Around this period,
no one believed that language models
were a pathway to artificial
intelligence to do things that are
claimed now of you know thinking and
reasoning and all those things that we
now associate with large language
models. It was taken as axiomatic that
to get to these higher level AI goals
that we needed to be
dealing with reasoning and planning and
abstract formal representations of
meaning and all of these kind of things.
There was no hope for this in language
models. It certainly wasn't because
language models weren't well known. um
that you know the concept of a language
model was really invented in 1913 by
Andre Marov. um so this is normally in
stats referred to as the invention of
Markoff models but what he actually
explored in this paper was consonant vow
transition probabilities in Alexander
Pushkin's novel Eugene Anagen. So um in
NLP terms this was a class-based
character biogram language model. But
where things really took off um is in
Claude Shannon's work um 1948 to 1951
where he explicitly explored building
charact both character and word level
engram models. So engram models are
where based on a short number of words
you predict the next word. Um so hence
you have markoff model technologies and
developing a lot of the formal basis of
information theory um that we still use
today. So anytime you're sitting in
pietorch and you're sticking in um
training with a cross entropy loss,
right? Cross entropy loss is something
that was introduced by Shannon. Um but
maybe the prior work that obser deserves
the most credit um for building up the
concept of language models is work in
Fred Gelan's group at IBM um in the
mid70s. So actually referring to
probabilistic models of next word
prediction as language models. Um that
was a terminology that was invented um
in Fred Gelanex's group and they were
the ones that initially showed you could
do all sorts of useful NLP tasks with
that language model technology. But the
useful tasks they had in mind started as
low-level tasks. spelling correction,
speech recognition, part of speech
tagging. They did get into machine
translation, which is starting to look
like an understanding task, but still
the general belief was this wasn't going
to give us natural language
understanding and artificial
intelligence. It was going to give us
string transduction mechanisms.
And to actually get to artificial
intelligence, we need all the other
stuff of AI, knowledge representations,
planning systems, reasoning um with
formal systems and things like that.
Um so how did all of this change? Um, I
was having dinner with some 20-year-olds
the other day and um, for what AI was
all about and how to make it better. Um,
they all believe that making progress on
AI was all about data. And I think that
story they've sort of picked up from the
story that's told about computer vision,
which looks like this. um that for most
of the history of computer vision 1950
to about 2004
um people did um experiments on
perception with minute amounts of often
hand constructed data and the field went
not very far and then in the mid 2000s
there started to be small modern style
machine learning labeled vision data
sets like Pascal Caltech 101 and CR 10
and 100 and that started to lead to
modern computer vision. And then the
whole world changed and we got to
working computer vision when imageet
came along. We s had millions of images,
a thousand classes, big data and object
classification in images was essentially
solved. But if we look at the
corresponding story for NLP, it's
actually a bit of a different story. So
here's the sort of story of large
language models. Um so as far as I'm
aware the very first paper that has in
it the triagram large language model is
this um paper in an obscure Taiwanese
conference from 1998
CPAT treebased language models with an
application for text verification in
Chinese. Um its main concern was how you
could do the diskbased indexing of a 200
millionword corpus. It's not very
interesting for how things developed in
large language models. But just a couple
of years after that, um, Yoshua Benjio
and colleagues invented the first neural
um, probabilistic language model. Um,
and so that was really the foundation of
when where where we got to today. But
their problem was that they just didn't
have enough compute. And because they
didn't have enough compute, they had to
use a small vocab, a small amount of
training um data and a small neural
network. So they suffered from
smallness. But then if we skip ahead a
few years to 2007,
we actually see a very different story.
Um so a group at Google including Jeff
Dean, you'll know um wrote a paper which
has large language models in the title.
large language models in machine
translation. And they trained a large
language model already in 2007 over a
two trillionword corpus. That's like um
modern scale. And this large language
model was fantastically useful. Any of
us that were involved in doing things
like machine translation in the 2000s
decade, we used this language model
because we didn't have enough compute to
build our own at that scale. and it
improved our results dramatically, but
it didn't lead to the modern revolution
um in large language models. And why
didn't it? The reason it didn't was they
were still working in the world of
engram language models, predicting the
next word based simply on the previous
four words. Um simple tabular based
probab probabilities with some complex
smoothing that I won't explain. And so
their problem was that they didn't have
enough model flexibility.
In 2018, the transformer had been
invented. So now we have a very
flexible, scalable, powerful model and
that led to the beginning of the modern
large language model era. Um GPT and the
much better known BERT model um were
then released. But these models were
only built over a 3.3 billion token
corpus. So it was really at this point
that the size of the data was the
restriction on getting further. And then
just a couple of years later, we then
started to see the 100 plus billion
parameter large language models being
released trained on trillions of tokens
including GPT3 and GPT4. And it's at
that point that large language models
then went bru. Um
and so that I think is the language
story that it's really this complete
trinity of compute model flexibility and
data that was necessary and really you
know we had the data much earlier. It
was actually needing the breakthrough of
getting enough model flexibility with
transformers that led to the takeoff.
And I'll come back to that theme in a
few minutes. But, you know, that's the
technology story. I think the even
bigger change that needed was a
philosophical turn leading to large
language models.
Um in 2013 at CIKM
um a workshop of that was the AKBC um
workshop and at that I presented a paper
of text knowledge and at that paper I
was trying to argue for what was then a
controversial position which was to say
well what most of the field is trying to
do is say we need to build up knowledge
resources we need to build up knowledge
graphs and um or more formal first order
knowledge and things like that. So we
could do knowledge and reasoning. Maybe
we shouldn't have to do that. Maybe we
should just say that human language text
is a knowledge encoding and we could
work directly um from that human um
language text. um and well of course I
didn't have the technology of neural
transformers um at my disposal but you
know that's ultimately what's proven to
be right that whereas for decades AI was
convinced it needed formal
representations and formal reasoning
systems what we've ended up with is
human language as the knowledge
representation and human language
thinking as how we do our reasoning. Um,
and so that's required a big change. Um,
so um,
I think for most of my audience, I can
see a few people more of my age out
there in the audience, but for most of
the audience, it's a younger audience.
So, you know, if you're under 30, um,
you don't remember a time when there
wasn't the worldwide web, and you
probably don't remember a world when
there wasn't Google. Um so I thought I'd
just also take a minute that actually um
in this talk back in 2013 I quoted some
remarks from Jeremy Zawadney in a blog
and I think it gives a character of how
the world used to be. So um Jeremy
Zawadney was an engineer at Yahoo um an
earlier company um in the 2000s um he
worked on MySQL and open-source software
at Yahoo. He programmed in Pearl if
anyone remembers Pearl. Um after that he
actually went and worked at Craigslist.
So he just retired last year actually.
Um but um this I think this gives a kind
of a character of this how we've had
this transformation um to a human
language knowledge world. So Jeremy
Zodney said, "Back in the late '9s when
I was building things that passed for
knowledge management tools at Marathon
Oil, there was all this talk about
knowledge workers. These were people
who'd have vast quantities of knowledge
at their fingertips. All they needed was
ways to organize, classify, search, and
collaborate. I think we've made it. But
the information isn't organized like I
had envisioned envisioned a few years
ago. It's just this big ugly mess known
as the web. Lots of pockets of
information from mailing lists, web
blogs, communities, and company websites
are loosely tied together by hyperlinks.
There's no grand schema or central
database. There's little structure or
quality control, no global vocabulary.
But even with all that going against it,
it's all indexed and easily searchable,
thanks largely to Google and the
companies that preceded it. Altveista,
Yahoo, etc. Most of the time it actually
works. Amazing.
And really large language models are the
next generation of this transition. um
where they have been able to take all of
this huge big ugly mess known as the web
and then process it and learn from it in
such a way that they can produce text
and reason as we're now familiar with.
And so that's led to the surprising
victory of NLP. Um it might seem strange
now where um even here at KD and D at
KDD there are tons of talks about large
language models. Um but you know it it
wasn't always so. Um, let me tell you
that if you're an NLP person back in the
1990s or even the 2000s decades, you
were really on the margins of the
machine learning and the AI community.
You know, areas fields like computer
vision, they were pretty central um
because that was kind of sort of clear
featurization and machine learning that
machine learning um community
understood. But you know, NLP people had
weird stuff. they had all of these word
tokens and then they want to put funny
data structures over them like trees and
graphs and things like that and nobody
wanted to deal with that stuff. Um we
were really on the margins of machine
learning and AI. Um, so it g it gives me
at least a little bit of a chuckle um
that here we are in the um 2020s and
when people talk about AI they really
mean natural language processing
technologies for most of the broader
world large language models are now our
AI. Um and so the surprising
philosophical change there is that this
informal
ubiquitous
probably 100,000 years old. Technology
invented by humans of human languages
has completely won over the formal
languages of recent centuries that
everyone assumed would be essential in
starting to build artificial
intelligence systems.
And what you've been able to do with
these systems is just amazing. So in the
last few years, we've seen these
extraordinary developments where large
language model systems can do really
clever stuff in math, science, and
coding. And again, I think it's sort of
worth reflecting on just how
extraordinary this is. I mean, so here's
the kind of um math level five problems
from this math data set. So that they're
sort of like math olympiate level
questions. Um the idea that you could
build a next token prediction system and
you could just feed in a problem like
this and it's going to give you the
answer.
I mean still kind of blows my mind even
as an NLP person. So it's proved to be
an extraordinarily powerful and scalable
technology and it's now scaled pretty
much all the way to the top. Um so just
um the other week um in the 2025
International Math Olympiad um the
Google Deep Mind um team um reached the
milestone of producing a gold medal
score. Um so being at the same level as
the very top um young human contestants
and so the IMO president commented their
solutions were astonishing in many
respects. IMO graders found them to be
clear, precise and most of them easy to
follow. But you know to look a bit more
in detail at this Google or not much
detail but two aspects of the Google D9
system I want to mention is first again
the surprising victory of natural
language processing in their earlier
work as recently as last year's Google
deep mind u math olympiad system they
were still trying to use um formal
reasoning languages and formal reasoning
methods as in alpha proof alpha geometry
making use of the lean formalism for
doing more formal solving where in IMO
2025
um they're just using um Gemini large
language model with some additional
special training data thrown in to have
more math training data. The other thing
I wanted to mention is if you remember
Tong um who did the neural attention um
back in 2015 um he's actually leading
the group that did this. Um so uh
congratulations to him.
Okay. Um so large language models um are
amazing. Have they given us artificial
intelligence?
Um and so that leads into what is
artificial intelligence? Um well the
artificial part is easy. So John
McCarthy who coined the term artificial
intelligence he defined it as the
science and engineering of making
intelligent machines. Okay, so that's
fine. But this definition is a cheat um
because it defines artificial
intelligence in terms of intelligence
without ever explaining intelligence. So
what is intelligence? Um and that part's
a bit more complex. Um so I come here
from the California Republic and so the
California legislature has been involved
in various AI bills. You might have
heard some of the controversy over the
best known one, SB 1047, which did not
pass. But the California legislator
legislature did pass a whole bunch of
other AI bills. And in one of them, um,
AB2885,
they provided a definition of artificial
intelligence. Artificial intelligence
means an engineered or machine-based
system that varies in its level of
autonomy and that can for explicit or
implicit objectives infer from the input
it receives how to generate outputs that
can influence physical or virtual
environments. Now I think this is an
awful definition of artificial
intelligence. um in particular um it
fails um a useful test for this which is
the landmine test. Now, it's not that
I'm wanting to advocate the use of
landmines, but I think it's important to
realize um that if you take this
definition and you compare an engineered
or machine-based system check that
varies in its level of autonomy,
landmines are definitely autonomous. And
that can for explicit or implicit
objectives, I think it's pretty explicit
what the objective of a landmine is,
infer from the input it receives, you
step on it. Um, how to generate outputs
that can influence physical or virtual
environments. It definitely influences
physical environments when you step on
it. Okay. So, I I think though we don't
really want to say that landmines are
intelligent.
Um, so it has to be harder than that.
Um, how else do people think about
intelligence? Um, so I think commonly
people confuse
being knowledgeable with intelligent.
So, you know, people say things about,
"Oh, my tax lawyer is really smart. He
knows all of the tax code backwards and
how to find deductions." But that's not
really intelligence. That's having a lot
of knowledge, right? And this is a sense
of well Wikipedia has a lot of
knowledge. Do we want to say it's
intelligent? Um then some people
particularly from an economics
background sort of think suggested in
the ability to do tasks. Um so that
artificial general intelligence that has
been defined as the ability to perform
most cognitive or economically valuable
tasks at or above the level of the
average person. Um, if this is how you
define AGI, I think we'll actually get
to it pretty soon. But I think this is
kind of understating human intelligence.
Like I think the unfortunate fact is
that most of the work that most human
beings do is actually pretty routine and
repetitive. And if you're doing a
routine and repetitive job, then yeah,
we're probably going to be able to get a
long way in automating many of those
white collar work jobs. And that's, you
know, important to know if you're an
economist. But is that really
intelligence?
Um, so early in the history of the
Stanford's human- centered artificial
intelligence um institute, I somehow got
tasked with writing some AI definitions.
And so this is the definition I wrote
for intelligence. um the ability to
learn and perform a range of techniques
to solve problems and achieve goals
appropriate to the context in an
uncertain ever varying world. Um now I
still think this is a pretty good um
definition of intelligence. Some people
have qualms with it. Um, you know, I
showed it to um an English professor who
was interested in AI, Michelle Elim, and
her reaction was that's very
mechanistic, and I don't think that was
a compliment. Um, but, you know, I think
it has a couple of really important
properties here. It stresses that you
know what's important to intelligence is
the ability to learn and that you have a
varying world where the world changes in
new unexpected ways and you have to be
able to learn to cope with new
conditions and I think that's what will
actually be seen to be the fundamental
quality of intelligence and if you look
think of things in that way actually our
large language models don't have much
intelligence at all. They've got a
certain ability to interpolate across
what they've seen. But by and large,
they are good because they have enormous
knowledge. They know everything that
human beings have discovered over the
last couple of millennia and they can
produce it at will, but they're not
actually very good at thinking about
different circumstances that haven't
been seen before.
And so with to actually get to greater
intelligence, I think we're going to
have to start to look at models that can
interact in a world and start to
understand causality and how you might
succeed in acting in a changing world
and still do things successfully. So
that's essentially what little kids are
so good at, right? that when they're,
you know, kicking over blocks and making
a mess of your household, what they're
doing is playing around to discover how
the world works and so they can deal
with a different world. That's a
viewpoint that's been emphasized by
Allison Gopnik and colleagues in their
book, The Scientist in the Crib, that
humans learn by interaction to infer
causality and to build up um an
understanding of how the world works.
And that's genuinely intelligence.
Okay, time is running away from me, but
I so I should um actually get to some
recent research of mine. So I wanted to
mention um some of
um two threads of recent research I've
been involved in. So one of them is
looking further at neural architectures
and training processes. So, so much of
what the big AI companies have been
doing is using the playbook of let's
make our training data larger and better
quality. Let's make our models bigger
and we will have further progress and
that works. Throw compute data and money
and at things and you can it's proving
that you can get a long way. But
remember that this field took off
through the development of a very
powerful neural network architecture,
the transformer. So I think we also
still need continued research on better
neural architectures. And so this is
some work that Robert Chisen colleagues
have been doing on trying to improve the
model of transformers.
So the starting off observation is we'd
like to have more systematic
generalization in our models thinking
and reasoning. And an obstacle to that
is that in a standard transformer
architecture um they achieve their
efficiency by doing just a compute
forward um computation. And what that
means is if you have some transformer
component that does something useful
like knows how to add up two numbers
that you can't get away with just one
copy of it in your neural network
because if you could add up 5 + 3 to get
8 but then you want to add two to it to
get 10 that the next addition operation
has to happen at a higher level of the

be repeated again and again up the

um share the same weights at every layer
and so they're sort of recurrent in

because since we have the same circuits

and indeed it already been shown um by

over sort of formal tasks with PCFGs and

trying to expand on that to have a
variant of a universal transformer
that'll actually scale to give us a
wellperforming um better form of
transformer large language model. And so
to do that we combine four ideas. So the
first is a particular kind of mixture of
experts at the multilayer perceptron
part of a transformer layer. So there's
lots of people are now used replacing um
the MLPS of a transformer um with
mixture of experts. This is a particular
way of doing the mixture of experts
where the network that decides which net
which experts to route to is built so
they're individual sigmoids um on the
selection procedure. And so therefore
the different experts don't compete with
each other. And the argument is um that
that's actually gives you a much better
way of learning the selection of
experts.
But then we further extend the mixture
of experts idea down to the attention
layer. So typically mixture of expert
models are only at the multi-layer
perceptron level. Um but by also using
experts um in the um OMV projections at
the attention level we can drastically
reduce the number of heads at that level
as well. But then for this particular
piece of work there were two new ideas
and the first one is actually a really
interesting observation that is a flaw
that comes about in um universal
transformers up to now. So in mo this is
getting a bit more in the weeds but in
modern large language models basically
everyone has moved to pre-layorm
transformers because they've been shown
to work better but with respect to
universal transformers there's a problem
and that is that in prelayer norm norm
transformers you're repeatedly adding on
to the residual and so the size of the
numbers in the residual stream is
getting bigger and bigger. So the norm
of the residual stream is growing over
layers. So for a regular transformer
that's kind of inelegant but isn't
really problematic because what happens
is that the numbers in the MLP at each
higher layer they also just get bigger
and so the scale of them matches each
other and things are fine. But if you're
trying to do a universal transformer,
this kills you because it's not possible
to have um repeated weights at the right
scale. They're too big at the low layers
and they're too small at the high
layers. So we need to fix that in some
way. And so um Robert came up with an
idea that he called perlayer norm where
effect effectively there is no layer
norm on the residual stream. Um and
instead the layer norm is just in the
part that is um going through um the
attention and the mixture of experts
layers. And so this avoids the growth of
the residual stream.
And then there's this final idea which
is also important. Um perhaps the
original idea of universal transformers
where you just have one set of weights
that's repeated at each transformer
layer is too naive. It seems like it's
too naive um both from you know loosely
the the formal details of neural
